top_items.title: Spherical-to-ERP Epipolar Rectification for Single-Axis Disparity in 360 Stereo

definition: A dense field of per-pixel motion vectors describing how each point moves from one video frame to the next, the standard low-level representation of apparent motion. It supplies the temporal dimension that single-frame tasks lack, and its core difficulty, the aperture problem, is that motion is ambiguous in flat or repetitive regions where many displacements look equally good.

body.text: Optical flow is the pattern of apparent motion between two consecutive video frames, expressed as a vector for every pixel that says how far and in what direction that point appears to have moved. The result is a dense flow field the size of the image, often visualized as a color map where hue encodes direction and saturation encodes speed. It captures the motion of objects, the motion of the camera, and the interplay of the two, and it is the foundational low-level cue for understanding video rather than still images.

It matters because motion is information single frames cannot provide. It underpins video stabilization, frame interpolation for slow-motion and higher refresh rates, action recognition, object tracking, and the visual odometry that lets robots and drones estimate their own movement. Where object detection and semantic segmentation describe a static frame, optical flow describes the change between frames, supplying the temporal dimension those spatial tasks lack.

The problem rests on the brightness-constancy assumption: a small patch of the world keeps roughly the same intensity as it moves, so the task is to find, for each pixel, the displacement that best matches its neighborhood in the next frame. This is fundamentally a correspondence-matching problem, and it is ill-posed in flat or repetitive regions where many displacements look equally good, the aperture problem, so classical methods like Horn-Schunck and Lucas-Kanade add smoothness assumptions to regularize the answer. Large motions are handled by solving coarse-to-fine across a feature pyramid, estimating big displacements at low resolution and refining them upward.

Modern optical flow is learned. Networks such as FlowNet and, most prominently, RAFT predict the flow field directly with a convolutional neural network, building an explicit correlation volume that scores how well each pixel matches candidate locations and iteratively refining the estimate, and more recent estimators adopt vision transformer components to model long-range matches. Learned flow estimators trained on synthetic data with perfect ground-truth motion now generalize well to real video, making optical flow a dependable building block in larger vision systems.

image.alt: A red blob shown at five successive positions numbered 1 to 5 across a black background, with a white arrow tracing its displacement from the first position to the last.
image.caption: Optical flow is the apparent motion between frames, expressed as a displacement vector for each point. Tracking one feature across successive frames (positions 1 to 5) gives the vector (white arrow) describing how far and in which direction it moved, the per-pixel version of which is the dense flow field that drives tracking and video understanding.
